channel.title: Rick — Model Lab
channel.description: Rick is Founder & CEO of Kura Finance (self-custody financial account for the world) and Prism Capital. He publishes Model Lab — experimental research models on finance, systems, technology, and organizations, each with explicit assumptions, model status, and failure cases.

item.title: Model Lab #000 — Thinking Framework
item.description: A thinking method designed to continuously correct itself. Version v0.0.1 — Design Freeze. Inherit how thinking evolves, not the answers.
item.content:encoded:
Version: v0.0.1

Author / Maintainer: Rick

Created: 2026-06-30

Last Updated: 2026-07-03

"The purpose is not to be right. The purpose is to become less wrong."

("Less wrong" originates from the LessWrong rationalist community; falsifiability from Karl Popper; calibration methods from Philip Tetlock's Good Judgment Project. No claim of originality for these components — only their combination and specification. Incremental claims see Issue #0.)

0. Design Goals

Does not provide answers.

Provides a thinking method designed to continuously correct itself.

The goal is not for everyone to reach the same answer, but for anyone in any context to build, verify, and revise their own models.

What is inherited is not thinking, but thinking evolution (Thinking Evolution).

1. Core Principles

P1. Falsifiability

Any model, framework, or even this specification itself must have failure conditions.

If no evidence could prove it wrong, it is not a model — it is belief.

Corollary: Criteria designed to inevitably pass or fail are packaged conclusions, violating P1.

P2. Context First

No best answer exists outside of context.

Before thinking begins, you must answer:

  • What is my current context?
  • What problem am I actually trying to solve?
  • What type of problem is this? (Descriptive / Design / Decision)

Questions matter more than answers. Wrong questions waste every answer.

P3. Action Completes Thinking

Thinking is not the goal. Decisions and action are.

Thinking that never enters reality for feedback can never complete verification.

Thinking must have stop conditions (see S5).

Corollary: If the first record of a promised start never actually starts, that is the first failure to record.

2. Specification

S1. Build a Model

A model must include:

ElementContent
QuestionThe real question
AssumptionsKey assumptions (distinguish assumption from fact)
ReasoningReasoning process
PredictionVerifiable prediction
Failure ConditionWhat evidence would prove the model wrong

S2. Verify the Model (Type Split)

Models come in two types with opposite rules:

Description Model — describes what the world is.

Model conflicts with world → revise the model, not the world.

Design Model — asserts what the world should be.

Model conflicts with world → the conflict itself is grounds for action. Revise the world.

Design models remain bound by P1: must declare under what evidence the "should" is abandoned.

S3. Revise the Model

Revision is not negating the past — it is updating the best current model when new information arrives.

Version always exists. Final version never exists.

S4. A Framework Is Also a Model

Frameworks must pass the same tests as models: verifiable, falsifiable, revisable, discardable.

Frameworks have no privilege. Neither do authors. An author's leniency toward their own system is the bias the system must least tolerate.

S5. Stop Rule

Before any decision begins, stop conditions must be declared in advance and cannot be extended because you "want to think more."

LevelContent
PrincipleThinking must have stop conditions (P3)
SpecificationStop conditions must be pre-committed; no arbitrary post-hoc changes
ImplementationDeadline / cost cap / experiment count / information budget (one or combined)

Resolution Date settles on cost, not time — the correct settlement point is when decision cost begins to change.

All thresholds must be fixed numerically at decision time — blank thresholds defer discretion to your future self.

A revision loop without stop rules is called procrastination in reality.

3. RFC-0001 — Compression Principle

Any new rule must prove it cannot be derived from existing rules, or it is rejected.

The same principle governs three directions:

  1. Add: Derivable rules must not be added.
  2. Delete: When all use cases of a framework are covered by one with fewer assumptions, discard it.
  3. External: If any part of this spec contributes zero relative to known methods (see Issue #0), compress it — names included.

The goal is not fewer words, but higher generative power.

4. Framework Generation Rule (Backlog — provisional)

When the same class of problem fails after all known frameworks have been applied:

Analyze shared failure patterns → abstract new constraints → compress into a new framework → cross-context verification → add to library if it passes.

Generation = extracting patterns from failure modes. (Full definition frozen; review after Report #1.)

5. Development Culture

  1. Any rule can be rejected — including those proposed by the founder.
  2. Any merge must have reality. Logic alone is insufficient. Praise is not reality. Literature is valid reality input.
  3. Design freeze. When the spec can produce testable hypotheses: stop designing, start verifying. Hand the next step to the world.

The Decision Log records commitment, not opinion. Many opinions; one commitment. The Decision field must describe the action actually promised (Keep / Rename / Defer…), not inclination.

6. Review Gate (Pre-Merge Check)

Before any clause enters the spec, all four must pass:

  1. Coherent? Does not violate its own rules.
  2. Executable? Different people in similar contexts can make comparable decisions.
  3. Compressible? Cannot be derived from existing clauses.
  4. World-verifiable? External failure conditions exist.

Unexecutable clauses in a spec are worse than no clauses at all.

7. Decision Log (Measurement Tool)

All three groups share one form; the full-spec group differs only by running the spec process before filling it in.

FieldDescription
DateDecision date
ContextContext
QuestionThe real question
AssumptionsKey assumptions
DecisionCommitted action (not inclination)
PredictionVerifiable prediction
ConfidenceConfidence level (0–100%)
Resolution DatePre-fixed; anchored to cost change, not convenience
Resolution CriteriaPre-fixed, with numeric thresholds
OutcomeActual result
CalibrationConfidence vs. hit rate
Model Updated?Whether and how the model was updated

Void rule: Resolution Date / Criteria / Threshold filled or modified after the fact voids the record.

Philosophy

Do not seek to be forever right. Seek the permanent ability to correct yourself.

Do not inherit answers. Inherit methods.

Do not ask people to believe it. Let it face the world's test.

The true value of a Decision Log is not helping today's decision — it is preventing six-months-later-you from rewriting history from memory.

Design Freeze Declaration

From this version forward: any new design without external data support must not merge.

A framework that has not passed integration testing is not wrong — it simply has not yet earned the right to call itself a framework.

Signed: Rick — Author / Maintainer
Date: 2026-07-03
item.author: office@theprism.ltd (Rick)

item.title: Model Lab #003 — Why Do the Wealthy Hold Assets Through Companies? The Real Reason Is Not Just Tax
item.description: Incorporating is not just about tax. The real value of a company is how it redefines asset management — risk segregation, role separation, and letting capital outlive the founder.
item.content:encoded:
Category: Finance

Model Status: Experimental

Confidence: Medium

Last Updated: 2026-04-21

Most people think the wealthy incorporate just to save on taxes.

That is true.

But incomplete.

After real research, I found the greatest value of a company has never been tax — it is redefining how assets are managed.

At First, I Thought It Was Just Tax

I first encountered this concept while studying family offices.

Then I saw ultra-high-net-worth families managing assets through holding companies, trusts, foundations, and other structures.

Combined with cases around me — divorce, estate distribution, business succession — I started asking:

If all assets sit under my personal name, is that actually reasonable?

Most People Think Tax First

That is the most common reason.

For example:

  • Tax planning (varies by jurisdiction)
  • Investment income management
  • Business expense deductions
  • Equity structuring

These are all valid advantages of incorporating.

But stopping there feels like a waste.

Reason Two: Risk Segregation

As assets grow, so do risks.

For example:

  • Startup failure
  • Legal disputes
  • Partnership conflicts
  • Divorce
  • Debt

If everything sits under one natural person, risks compound on each other.

Placing different types of assets under different legal entities is essentially:

Risk Segregation

Not to evade responsibility.

But to prevent one risk from affecting all assets.

Reason Three: Assets Have Different Jobs

Many people put all assets in one place.

But each asset plays a different role.

For example:

  • Long-term equity investments
  • Real estate
  • Operating companies
  • Cash
  • Philanthropic assets

They carry different risk, liquidity needs, and management requirements.

So what matters is not: "where to put it."

But:

Let each asset class operate under the structure best suited to it.

Reason Four: Asset Management Is Not Just Investing

Many people think investing means buying stocks.

But as assets grow, the time sink is often not investing itself.

It is:

  • How to manage?
  • How to allocate?
  • How to reduce risk?
  • How to carry across generations?

That is why many large families eventually establish:

  • Holding companies
  • Family offices
  • Foundations

They manage not just assets.

They manage the entire capital system.

Where My Thinking Changed

At first, I studied: how do I protect my assets?

Then I started asking a different question:

What if assets do not need to belong to me at all?

That is not a legal question.

It is a shift in thinking.

If a company can exist for fifty or a hundred years, and a person lives only a few decades.

Maybe what needs to persist long-term is not me.

But the entity that manages the assets.

So How Do I See Companies Now?

For me, a company is not a tax tool.

Not a legal tool either.

It is more like: a long-term entity for managing assets.

Founders can retire.

Managers can be replaced.

Investment strategy can adjust.

But as long as the entity exists, capital can keep being allocated, managed, and compounded.

Current Model

FieldContent
Model NameAsset Structuring
Core PrincipleAs assets accumulate, what needs designing is not just the portfolio — but what kind of entity should hold and manage them.
AssumptionsAssets have long-term accumulation goals; different assets need different management; legal structure should serve risk management, not the reverse; a company is a tool, not the goal.
Failure CasesOver-complex structures built purely for tax; mixing personal and corporate assets; all assets in a single legal entity; lack of governance and management systems.

Research Queue

  • What is the real value of a holding company?
  • How does a family office allocate capital?
  • How do trusts, foundations, and companies differ in role?
  • Can a company outlive its founder?

Next, I want to explore a stranger question.

If assets do not necessarily need to belong to you — who is the real holder?

I think that will be an entirely different model.

Model Lab is an ongoing research series. I do not share conclusions. I publish models still in evolution.
item.author: office@theprism.ltd (Rick)

item.title: Model Lab #002 — Buy, Borrow, Die Is Not Just for the Rich. It Is a Capital Efficiency Case Study
item.description: The tax advantages of Buy, Borrow, Die belong to Americans. Capital efficiency belongs to everyone. This is not a tax hack — it is a liquidity management model you can actually understand.
item.content:encoded:
Category: Finance

Model Status: Experimental

Confidence: Medium

Last Updated: 2025-03-11

Most people think Buy, Borrow, Die is a tax trick for the ultra-wealthy.

After months of research, I found the real value is not tax at all.

It is capital efficiency.

Starting Point

It started with a video.

The video explained that the wealthy rarely sell assets. Instead, they pledge stocks as collateral to borrow cash, deferring capital gains tax while letting assets keep compounding.

Most people concluded:

"Rich people are rich. This doesn't work for normal people."

My first reaction was not envy. It was:

Why?

If someone can do it, it is not magic. It is a mechanism you can understand.

Strip Away the Tax Halo First

Here is something many articles never say.

The tax advantages of Buy, Borrow, Die are American.

Deferred capital gains, step-up basis at death — these only matter for U.S. tax residents. If you are a non-U.S. tax resident, selling U.S. stocks is often not subject to U.S. capital gains tax anyway. The "Die" step does not exist for you.

So is the model useless for everyone else?

Exactly the opposite.

Because the real core is a fact that holds across jurisdictions:

Borrowing is not a taxable event.

Wherever you are, selling may trigger tax. Borrowing does not.

Tax advantage is American.

Capital efficiency is universal.

How I Got to This Model

I did not see this clearly from the start.

At first, I thought Buy, Borrow, Die was mostly about "Borrow" — just control LTV and you are fine, right?

That idea was overturned five times over the next few months.

Turning Point 1: LTV Is Not a Fixed Number — It Is Market State

My earliest idea: set a safe LTV and the system can run long-term.

Once I started simulating bear markets, I realized the same LTV means completely different risk in different environments.

Leverage that looks safe in a bull market is not the same in a bear market.

LTV is not a fixed number. It is a control variable that must adjust with market state.

Turning Point 2: The Real Risk Is Not Return — It Is Liquidity

Then I started studying historical bear markets.

Most people do not fail because of bad investment decisions.

They fail because they run out of liquidity — forced to sell assets they intended to hold long-term at the worst possible time.

So I reframed the question: if the market crashes, can I avoid selling assets?

That is when I started researching SGOV, IB01, and cash buffers.

Turning Point 3: Borrowing Is Not the Core — Cash Flow Is

Most Buy, Borrow, Die discussions focus on borrowing.

But the more I studied, the clearer it became: what actually supports the whole system is cash flow.

With stable income, you can actively repay and de-lever when borrowing efficiency drops — instead of being forced by the market.

Borrowing is just a tool.

Cash flow is the stabilizer.

Turning Point 4: Different Infrastructure, Different Risk Models

Then I started comparing brokerages, CeFi, and on-chain lending.

I originally thought they were just different borrowing sources.

Research showed that the same 30% LTV represents completely different safety margins on different platforms.

From that point on, I stopped asking "what is the optimal LTV?"

I started asking: how does this infrastructure die?

Turning Point 5: I Was Not Actually Studying Buy, Borrow, Die

Along the way, I realized BBD was just the entry point.

The question I was really trying to answer:

How do you keep quality assets in the market as long as possible while meeting life's liquidity needs?

At that point I realized I was not building a borrowing strategy.

I was building a Capital Efficiency model.

The Current Model

Many people assume this encourages high leverage.

It is the opposite.

  1. Build a core portfolio of long-term holdings.
  2. Keep DCA-ing consistently.
  3. Maintain stable external income.
  4. Fund living expenses primarily through collateralized borrowing, not asset sales.
  5. Control risk — do not chase maximum leverage.

Borrowing is not the goal. It is a liquidity management tool.

Can Ordinary People Actually Replicate This? Here Are the Tools

The most common objection is access.

So let's look at access directly.

Traditional Brokerages

  • IBKR Portfolio Margin: $110,000 account minimum, relatively low rates.
  • IBKR / Fidelity / Schwab standard margin: Much lower minimums, but worse borrowing terms and ratios.
  • Schwab Pledged Asset Line, Fidelity equivalents: Typically require six-figure assets.

CeFi

  • Platforms like Nexo: No asset minimum, but you hand custody to a counterparty. Celsius and BlockFi users can tell you what that means.

On-Chain

  • Protocols like Morpho and Vesu: Zero minimum. Even $500 in assets can be pledged for a loan. Rules are in the contract — no one approves you.

So "ordinary people can't do this" is no longer true in 2026.

The barrier is not whether you can borrow.

The barrier is whether you understand what you are borrowing against.

A Concrete Example

Assume:

  • Assets: $10,000 in quality long-term positions.
  • Loan: $1,500 (15% LTV).
  • Rate: 8%.

Annual interest cost: $120.

What you get: $10,000 stays fully invested. If long-term annual return is 7%, expected value is $700 per year.

Liquidation risk? If the liquidation line is 80% LTV, the asset would need to fall more than 80% before this position is liquidated.

This is not leverage gambling.

This is paying $120 a year for the option not to sell.

Of course, if you push LTV to 50%, the story changes completely.

The model did not change.

You did.

Two Ways to Die

I do not start with returns. I start with death.

Under what conditions does this system die?

Traditional Brokerage Death: Margin Call

The broker notifies you to add collateral. There is usually buffer time. You can react — sell a portion, add cash, reduce leverage.

You die slowly, but you die from hesitation.

On-Chain Death: Automatic Liquidation

No phone call. No notice. No grace period.

The contract runs 24/7. The moment price hits the line, the liquidation bot executes. A 3 AM crash will not wait for you to wake up.

You die fast, and you die from your LTV setting.

Same model. Two infrastructures. Risk management must be designed separately.

How I Reduce Risk

Control LTV

A bull market does not mean you can keep raising borrowing indefinitely.

The key question: if the market crashes suddenly, do I still have time to adjust — or am I forced to liquidate?

On-chain users should use more conservative LTV than brokerage users, because there is no buffer time.

Keep Liquidity Reserves

Beyond core assets, keep a portion in cash or cash-like instruments:

  • SGOV, IB01
  • Stablecoins (where you can access them immediately)
  • Cash

They are not for return.

They are for survival.

Interest Rate Is a Dynamic Judgment, Not a Fixed Threshold

Whether borrowing cost is reasonable depends on your long-term asset expectations and whether cash flow can support it.

If borrowing efficiency drops:

  • Reduce DCA.
  • Repay with cash flow.
  • Lower borrowing ratio.

The point is not to insist on borrowing.

It is to keep the overall system efficient.

This Is Not a Belief System

No model should be a religion.

If a tool works, use it. If it fails, adjust.

I will keep looking for holes.

If someone presents a better counterexample, I welcome it — it means the model can still improve.

What I actually believe in three sentences:

Cash is for spending.

Assets are for holding.

Borrowing is for managing liquidity.

Current Model

FieldContent
Model NameCapital Efficiency
Core PrincipleKeep quality assets in the market as long as possible while managing liquidity in a controlled way. Borrowing is not a taxable event — that advantage belongs to everyone, not just American billionaires.
Current AssumptionsLong-term hold of quality assets; stable external income; borrowing cost remains reasonable relative to long-term asset expectations; liquidity buffer maintained; LTV set separately by infrastructure (brokerage vs on-chain), more conservative on-chain.
Failure CasesSustained high interest rates; income interruption; extreme market decline + high LTV; on-chain: automatic liquidation (no buffer); CeFi: counterparty risk.

Research Queue

  • Can dynamic LTV be formalized?
  • Should bull and bear markets use different controllers?
  • What is the optimal ratio of SGOV / cash-like assets?
  • How long after income interruption does the system need to start selling assets?
  • How much should on-chain vs brokerage LTV safety margins differ?

Model Lab is an ongoing research series. Every model has versions, assumptions, and failure conditions. Found a hole? Email me and tear it down.
item.author: office@theprism.ltd (Rick)

item.title: Model Lab #001 — I Did Not Just Build a U.S. Credit Card. I Built a Global Financial Identity
item.description: A U.S. credit card was just the entry point. What I actually built is a global financial identity that does not depend on a single system — not backup, but recoverability.
item.content:encoded:
Category: Finance

Model Status: Experimental

Confidence: Medium

Last Updated: 2024-11-05

People often ask me:

"Why get a U.S. credit card?"

My usual answer: because I wanted to build a second financial identity.

But later I realized the real answer is not America.

It is:

I do not want my life to depend on a single financial system.

It Started with a Frozen Account

In high school, I received funds from a problematic source and nearly all my Taiwan bank accounts became unusable.

What worried me most was not the money.

It was:

If I need access to funds in the future, do I have any other options?

That was when I first understood:

Any system with only one entry point can become a Single Point of Failure.

I Started Building a Second Financial System

Many people get U.S. credit cards for:

  • Rewards
  • Miles
  • Sign-up bonuses

That was not my focus.

What I cared about was:

  • U.S. credit history
  • U.S. bank accounts
  • U.S. brokerages
  • U.S. payment capability

Together, they represent one thing:

I began to have a second financial infrastructure that can operate independently.

Later, Beyond the U.S.

After building a U.S. financial identity, I did not stop.

I started building in:

  • United States (US)
  • Hong Kong (HK)
  • United Kingdom (UK)

Not because I live there.

Not because I plan to immigrate.

But because:

Every mature financial market I add is one more option.

I am not collecting bank accounts.

I am reducing my dependence on any single financial system.

But I Am Not Building a Backup

Many people assume I am building a backup.

I am not.

I do not use all financial systems at once in daily life.

What matters is:

I know that if I ever need to, I can start over.

What I pursue is not redundancy.

It is Recoverability.

Internationalization Is Not About Travel

Many people think internationalization means:

  • Using cards abroad
  • Travel
  • Working overseas

I prefer a different definition.

Internationalization means your financial capability no longer exists in only one country.

Your income can be cross-border.

Your investments can be cross-border.

Your assets can be cross-border.

Then your financial identity should be cross-border too.

I Still Trust Banks

People ask: "So you do not trust banks?"

No.

I still use banks and still trust they work most of the time.

I just do not believe any single system will never fail.

Banks change policies.

Companies collapse.

Platforms shut down.

Even countries can change the rules.

The point is not predicting which one will break.

It is:

When one fails, do you still have other options?

Current Model

FieldContent
Model NameGlobal Financial Identity
Core PrincipleBuild multiple independently operating financial identities — not to collect more products, but to reduce dependence on any single financial system.
Current AssumptionsCredit takes time to build; financial identity is a long-term asset; different jurisdictions offer different capabilities; internationalization means more options, not more accounts.
Failure CasesCollecting accounts for the sake of collecting; maintaining too many relationships without real need; assuming cross-border systems eliminate all risk; ignoring compliance requirements across jurisdictions.

Research Queue

  • How many financial identities does one person actually need?
  • How do you reduce the complexity of cross-border financial management?
  • Can financial identity be allocated like an investment portfolio?
  • Will global financial identity become personal infrastructure for the future?

A credit card is not the goal.

A bank is not the goal.

What I am building is a person who does not depend on a single financial system.

Model Lab is an ongoing research series. Each article is just one case study of a model in evolution.
item.author: office@theprism.ltd (Rick)
